name: kettlebell lunge pass through
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in front of your chest with both hands.
Take a step forward with your right foot, lowering your body into a lunge position.
As you lunge forward, pass the kettlebell under your right thigh and transfer it to your left hand.
Push off with your right foot to return to the starting position, while simultaneously passing the kettlebell back to your right hand.
Repeat the lunge on the opposite side, passing the kettlebell under your left thigh.
Continue alternating lunges and passing the kettlebell between hands for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a kettlebell in front of your chest with both hands. Take a step forward with your right foot, lowering your body into a lunge position. As you lunge forward, pass the kettlebell under your right thigh and transfer it to your left hand. Push off with your right foot to return to the starting position, while simultaneously passing the kettlebell back to your right hand. Repeat the lunge on the opposite side, passing the kettlebell under your left thigh. Continue alternating lunges and passing the kettlebell between hands for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Maintain neutral spine and avoid positions that aggravate the lower back.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
